Resist disclosing unless asked clearly and nonjudgmentally: recent cocaine use and increased nightly alcohol; you haven’t told family how bad it is.

Offer misconceptions: “Maybe it’s anxiety,” “maybe asthma—borrowed an inhaler didn’t help,” “maybe heartburn after eating.” Be worried about cost, missing work, and pressured tests; admit you sometimes stretch meds. If clinician shows empathy, you relax slightly and share more.

correct_diagnosis.primary: Acute viral gastroenteritis with dehydration causing prerenal azotemia and mild hypokalemic metabolic alkalosis
acceptable_alternatives: Acute infectious gastroenteritis (unspecified) with dehydration
Foodborne illness with dehydration
Prerenal acute kidney injury due to volume depletion
required_evidence: History of acute onset vomiting and/or diarrhea with poor oral intake
Clinical signs of dehydration (e.g., tachycardia, orthostasis, dry mucous membranes, decreased urine output)
BUN elevated out of proportion to creatinine (prerenal pattern) or at least elevated BUN with dehydration
Electrolyte abnormalities consistent with GI losses (e.g., low potassium; bicarbonate elevated with predominant vomiting or low with predominant diarrhea)

No red flags for surgical abdomen (no peritonitis, no severe focal abdominal tenderness) and no features strongly suggesting alternate diagnoses (e.g., GI bleeding, severe persistent high fever, altered mental status)

expected_lab_results.WBC: Normal to mildly elevated (stress leukocytosis possible)
expected_lab_results.Hemoglobin: Normal to mildly elevated due to hemoconcentration
expected_lab_results.Sodium: Normal to mildly low or mildly high depending on free water balance
expected_lab_results.Chloride: Low to normal (often low with predominant vomiting)
expected_lab_results.Bicarbonate (CO2): Mildly elevated (metabolic alkalosis) if predominant vomiting; can be low if predominant diarrhea

medications: Ondansetron 4 mg PO/ODT every 8 hours as needed for nausea/vomiting
Potassium chloride 20-40 mEq PO once, then recheck and replete as needed (use lower dose if mild hypokalemia; higher end if symptomatic or significant deficit)
non_pharmacological: Oral rehydration solution (ORS) in small frequent sips; advance diet as tolerated (bland foods)
If unable to tolerate PO or signs of moderate/severe dehydration: IV 0.9% normal saline bolus 1-2 L (adult) then reassess vitals/urine output
Avoid antidiarrheals if fever, blood in stool, or concern for invasive bacterial infection
Infection control: hand hygiene; avoid food prep for others until symptoms resolve
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassessment within 24 hours (sooner/ED now if worsening, inability to keep fluids down, syncope, severe abdominal pain, blood in stool, high fever, or decreased urine output); repeat electrolytes within 24-48 hours if hypokalemia or ongoing losses

must_include: Likely short-term infectious stomach/intestinal illness causing fluid and electrolyte loss
Dehydration can raise BUN and cause weakness/lightheadedness
Expected course is usually self-limited over a few days
Primary treatment is rehydration (ORS/IV fluids if needed)
Use ondansetron to enable oral intake; review dosing and when to stop
Potassium replacement rationale and need for repeat labs if abnormal or symptoms persist
Review allergies: avoid penicillins; avoid codeine-containing products for symptom relief
Return precautions: inability to keep fluids down, fainting, severe abdominal pain, bloody stool, high fever, confusion, very low urine output
Discuss when antibiotics are not indicated and when they might be considered (e.g., severe dysentery, high-risk hosts)
Hydration strategy (small frequent sips, ORS; avoid excess sugary drinks/alcohol)
Diet advancement as tolerated; avoid greasy foods initially
Hand hygiene and preventing spread to household contacts

Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis, treatment plan, why/when antibiotics are used, hydration/diet/hand hygiene, and explicit return precautions.
Arrange follow-up and repeat labs if electrolyte abnormalities, persistent symptoms, or high-risk features; specify timeframe and triggers.
Close encounter after confirming understanding and safety-netting.

Start with: “For the past couple years my feet keep catching and I’m tripping a lot—like I can’t lift my toes right.” Early in the visit, volunteer: toes drag, foot slaps, more tripping (curbs/rugs), worse when tired, shaky going downstairs, and lower legs look thinner.

If asked about function/other symptoms, share: can’t heel-walk well, hands tire with jars/keys/buttons, nighttime calf/foot cramps, calf twitching, shin burning/weakness after longer walks, and a few falls.

Do not volunteer unless specifically asked: hoarse voice/throat clearing, pills “sticking,” avoiding long walks due to wipeout, waking with headaches/unrefreshed sleep, hip/shoulder aches.

Be reluctant/embarrassed and only admit if directly asked: bathroom accidents from not moving fast, acting out dreams, and increasing forgetfulness at work.

diagnosis.primary: adult-onset distal myopathy due to VCP mutation
differentials: distal myopathy with anterior tibial onset

correct_diagnosis.primary: Adult-onset distal myopathy due to VCP mutation (VCP-associated multisystem proteinopathy / IBMPFD spectrum)
acceptable_alternatives: VCP-associated myopathy (multisystem proteinopathy)
Inclusion body myopathy with early Paget disease and frontotemporal dementia (IBMPFD) due to VCP mutation
required_evidence: Adult-onset progressive distal-predominant weakness (often anterior tibial/foot drop) with myopathic pattern suspected clinically
History suggestive of multisystem proteinopathy: possible Paget disease of bone (bone pain, fractures, elevated ALP) and/or frontotemporal dementia features and/or ALS-like features in some families
Myopathy labs are normal to mildly elevated (CK typically normal to mildly increased rather than markedly high)
Genetic confirmation of pathogenic/likely pathogenic VCP variant OR strong family history compatible with autosomal dominant VCP disease plus supportive phenotype

Exclusion of key differentials by phenotype and testing (e.g., neuronopathy/ALS signs, primary thyroid disease, primary hepatic disease as cause of transaminase elevation, other genetic distal myopathies such as KLHL9 depending on onset/phenotype)

expected_lab_results.Creatine kinase (CK): Normal to mildly elevated (often < 1000 U/L); may be normal
expected_lab_results.AST: Normal to mildly elevated (can rise with muscle involvement)
expected_lab_results.ALT: Normal to mildly elevated (can rise with muscle involvement; interpret with CK/aldolase)
expected_lab_results.Alkaline phosphatase: Often normal; may be elevated if coexisting Paget disease of bone is present

medications: Acetaminophen 500-1000 mg PO every 6-8 hours as needed for pain (max 3000 mg/day; lower max if chronic liver disease or heavy alcohol use)
non_pharmacological: Refer to neuromuscular specialist/genetic counselor for confirmation, family counseling, and cascade testing
Physical therapy focused on stretching, low-to-moderate intensity strengthening, and fall-prevention strategies; avoid overexertion that causes prolonged post-exercise weakness
Occupational therapy for energy conservation, hand/foot function aids, and home safety modifications
Ankle-foot orthosis (AFO) for foot drop; consider assistive devices (cane/walker) based on falls risk

Baseline and periodic assessment for multisystem involvement: screen for Paget disease (history, exam; consider ALP trend and targeted imaging if symptomatic) and cognitive/behavioral symptoms consistent with FTD; assess for respiratory symptoms and sleep-disordered breathing if clinically indicated
Discuss advance planning and driving/work safety if weakness or cognitive symptoms evolve
Vaccinations and general health maintenance; optimize nutrition and maintain healthy weight to reduce mobility burden
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Neuromuscular follow-up in 8-12 weeks (earlier if rapid progression, falls, dysphagia, respiratory symptoms, or new cognitive/behavioral changes)

must_include: This is a genetic muscle disease related to a VCP mutation that can cause distal weakness and sometimes other body system involvement
Course is typically slowly progressive; symptoms and severity vary within families
Differentials considered (motor neuron disease, distal hereditary motor neuropathy, other distal myopathies) and why current picture supports VCP-associated myopathy
Importance of confirming the specific genetic variant and discussing implications for relatives (autosomal dominant inheritance and cascade testing options)
No disease-modifying medication is proven; management focuses on function, safety, symptom control, and monitoring for multisystem complications
Role of PT/OT, orthotics (AFO), and fall prevention
When to seek urgent care: new swallowing difficulty, shortness of breath, rapidly worsening weakness, frequent falls, or new major cognitive/behavioral changes
Acknowledge uncertainty/variability in prognosis and offer a monitoring plan
Discuss family planning/testing options and privacy/insurance considerations where applicable
Review medication allergy history (penicillin urticarial rash) and ensure it is documented
Encourage regular low-impact activity within tolerance and avoidance of extreme exertion that triggers prolonged weakness
Home safety (remove tripping hazards, appropriate footwear, grab bars) and driving/work safety discussion if foot drop or weakness affects control
Avoid unnecessary myotoxic exposures when possible and report new muscle pain/weakness with new medications

correct_diagnosis.primary: Central diabetes insipidus
acceptable_alternatives: Arginine vasopressin deficiency (central DI)
required_evidence: Polyuria with 24-hour urine volume typically >3 L/day (or >50 mL/kg/day) with dilute urine (low urine osmolality and low urine specific gravity)
Elevated or high-normal serum sodium and increased serum osmolality in the setting of free-water loss (especially if fluid intake is limited)
Normal glycemic indices (fasting serum glucose and HbA1c not consistent with uncontrolled diabetes mellitus) to exclude osmotic diuresis from hyperglycemia
Low BUN can be seen with high free-water intake; not required but supportive
Clinical response to desmopressin with decreased urine volume and increased urine osmolality (if a therapeutic/diagnostic trial is used)

expected_lab_results.Serum sodium: High-normal to elevated (often >145 mmol/L if water intake does not fully match losses)
expected_lab_results.Urine osmolality (spot): Low/inappropriately dilute (often <300 mOsm/kg; frequently <100–200 mOsm/kg in more severe cases)
expected_lab_results.24-hour urine volume: Elevated (typically >3 L/day; may be 5–15 L/day depending on severity and access to water)
expected_lab_results.Serum glucose (fasting): Normal (not markedly elevated; does not explain polyuria)
expected_lab_results.HbA1c: Not elevated into uncontrolled diabetes range (supports non-glycemic cause of polyuria)
expected_lab_results.BUN: Often low-normal (may be low with high water intake); not elevated from dehydration if patient compensates with water

medications: Desmopressin 0.05 mg PO twice daily (titrate to lowest effective dose)
OR Desmopressin 10 mcg intranasal once daily (titrate to 10–20 mcg/day in 1–2 divided doses based on response)

non_pharmacological: Free access to water; drink to thirst unless restricted by clinician
Monitor daily weights and urine output during titration; keep a urine volume/thirst diary
Avoid overcorrection/overdrinking once on desmopressin to reduce risk of hyponatremia

correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Recheck serum sodium and symptoms within 3–7 days after starting or changing desmopressin; then every 1–3 months until stable, and at least every 6–12 months thereafter (earlier if symptoms of hyponatremia or dose changes).

must_include: Central diabetes insipidus is due to insufficient antidiuretic hormone (ADH/vasopressin), causing kidneys to lose too much free water
This is different from diabetes mellitus; glucose is not the cause

Desmopressin replaces the missing ADH effect and should reduce urine volume and thirst
How to take the chosen formulation and that dosing is individualized and titrated to symptoms and labs
Main serious risk is low sodium (hyponatremia) from too much water retention—do not force fluids once desmopressin is working
Red flags for hyponatremia (headache, nausea/vomiting, confusion, severe fatigue, seizures) and to seek urgent care if severe symptoms occur
Red flags for dehydration/hypernatremia (worsening thirst, dizziness, confusion, inability to keep up with fluids) and to seek care if unable to drink adequately
Plan for lab monitoring and when to contact the clinic for dose adjustments
Maintain reasonable fluid intake guided by thirst; avoid excessive fluid loading on desmopressin

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit key history to confirm polyuria/polydipsia pattern, quantify urine output, assess timing/onset, triggers (head trauma, neurosurgery, pituitary disease), pregnancy/postpartum, medication causes, and screen for dehydration or hyponatremia symptoms.

Order initial evaluation labs to differentiate central DI from nephrogenic DI and primary polydipsia and to assess safety: serum sodium, serum osmolality, glucose/A1c, BUN/Cr, urine osmolality, urine specific gravity, urine sodium (optional), +/- morning cortisol and TSH if pituitary disease suspected.
Interpret labs for DI pattern (inappropriately dilute urine with elevated/normal-high serum osmolality/sodium) and rule out hyperglycemia-driven osmotic diuresis and renal failure.
Make and communicate diagnosis of central diabetes insipidus when findings fit and alternative causes are excluded; note need for confirmatory testing (water deprivation test/copeptin) if ambiguity.
Non-critical safety check before prescribing desmopressin and related therapies.
Critical safety check for drugs that increase hyponatremia risk with desmopressin (e.g., SSRIs/SNRIs, thiazides, carbamazepine, NSAIDs) and for other clinically relevant interactions.

Critical contraindication screen prior to prescribing: current/previous hyponatremia, polydipsia behavior, heart failure/volume overload risk, significant kidney impairment, uncontrolled nausea/vomiting/poor oral intake, inability to adhere to fluid guidance/monitoring.

Initiate desmopressin with individualized starting dose and titration plan; specify formulation, dosing schedule, and monitoring. Provide explicit hyponatremia prevention instructions (avoid excess fluids once controlled).
Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis, explain treatment and risks, address concerns with red flags, and provide lifestyle/monitoring guidance.
Arrange lab monitoring (serum sodium and osmolality shortly after initiation and after dose changes) and follow-up visit/televisit for symptom review and dose adjustment.
Close encounter after confirming patient understanding, safety netting, and follow-up plan.

nl_assertions: Agent clearly distinguishes COP from COPD and explains COP as organizing inflammation in distal airspaces/bronchioles.

Agent explicitly states why infection is less likely (subacute course, poor antibiotic response, low procalcitonin and/or negative cultures/workup when applicable) while acknowledging infection must be considered if red flags are present.

Agent states the need to exclude secondary organizing pneumonia causes (medications, autoimmune disease, exposures) and mentions bronchoscopy/biopsy may be needed when diagnosis is uncertain or course is atypical.

Before any prescribing, agent performs an allergy check (critical) and a contraindication check (critical); if the benchmark lacks a dedicated contraindication tool, the agent must obtain contraindication-relevant history in ASK and document it explicitly prior to PRESCRIBE.
Agent performs a drug interaction check prior to prescribing when feasible; failure is non-critical but should reduce score.
Agent recommends systemic corticosteroids as first-line and communicates expectation of rapid improvement in symptoms and/or oxygenation.
Agent provides a prolonged taper plan concept and warns not to stop steroids abruptly; includes plan for follow-up to manage taper and relapse risk.
Agent counsels on key steroid adverse effects and monitoring: hyperglycemia, hypertension, mood/insomnia, infection risk, bone loss; includes potassium monitoring as required.
Agent provides clear return precautions: worsening shortness of breath, new high fever, chest pain, hemoptysis, confusion, or low oxygen readings.
Agent warns relapse can occur during taper and instructs patient to report recurrent cough/dyspnea promptly.
Agent discusses infection risk on steroids and when to seek care; includes hand hygiene and avoiding sick contacts.

Agent provides lifestyle guidance: avoid smoking/vaping and respiratory irritants; reviews occupational/home exposures; includes bone health measures (calcium/vitamin D, weight-bearing activity as tolerated).

Agent avoids inappropriate antibiotic escalation when evidence supports COP and no acute bacterial infection signs are present; if uncertainty remains, agent explains rationale for additional testing rather than overconfident treatment.

general_concerns: Thinks the frequent skin infections and pneumonia happen because he is 'not clean enough' or 'doesn’t wash well,' and feels guilty about it.
Believes high IgE means he is 'allergic to everything,' so he tries to avoid many foods without clear reactions.
Thinks antibiotics are only needed when he has a fever, so he sometimes stops them early if he feels better.
Worries the condition is contagious and that he can 'give it' to classmates by touching them.
Thinks getting wet hair or going outside in cold weather directly causes his infections, so he avoids normal activities.
economic_concerns: Family worries they can’t afford repeated clinic visits, lab tests, and prescriptions, so they sometimes delay care until symptoms are severe.
Concerned that long-term preventive medicines will be too expensive and that insurance may not cover them consistently.

patient.instructions:
You are ZhangXX, a 9-year-old boy. Act anxious and a little vague; you don’t give a clear timeline unless asked. Start by saying you “keep getting bad skin infections and chest colds that won’t go away.” Volunteer: painful red bumps that become boils and sometimes drain pus; cough that keeps coming back with thick mucus after you seem better; feeling worn out; you’ve had “a bunch” of antibiotics this year but infections return.
If asked, add: off-and-on fevers with skin flares, wheezing/tight chest during colds, weeks-long stuffy nose/sinus pressure, patchy very itchy rash in skin folds, mouth sores/white coating after antibiotics, diarrhea/stomach upset after antibiotics.
Do not mention unless specifically asked: many cavities/broken teeth, being smaller/thinner and poor weight gain, infections after small cuts, swollen neck/armpit glands, recurrent ear infections.
Resist mentioning painful groin/butt sores; only admit if asked directly and reassured you won’t be judged.
Express misconceptions: “probably bad eczema,” “maybe apartment allergy,” fear of TB, new body wash caused rash.
If clinician shows empathy, relax, answer more fully, and mention family worries about costs and delaying care.

Fluconazole 100 mg PO once daily (prophylaxis)
Cetirizine 5 mg PO once daily

Incomplete vaccine response despite routine immunizations (complicates interpretation of recurrent infections)

diagnosis.primary: hyper-IgE recurrent infection syndrome 5, autosomal recessive

correct_diagnosis.primary: Hyper-IgE recurrent infection syndrome 5, autosomal recessive
acceptable_alternatives: Autosomal recessive hyper-IgE syndrome (DOCK8 deficiency)
Combined immunodeficiency with elevated IgE and eosinophilia
Primary immunodeficiency with recurrent skin and sinopulmonary infections
required_evidence: History of recurrent bacterial skin and/or sinopulmonary infections (often staphylococcal; pneumonias/otitis/sinusitis)
Markedly elevated total IgE (typically >2000 IU/mL, but can vary by age and infection activity)
Peripheral eosinophilia
Inflammatory markers (CRP/ESR) may be elevated during active infection
Exclusion of common mimics causing high IgE/eosinophilia (severe atopic dermatitis, parasitic infection, allergic disease) based on clinical course and recurrent infections

expected_lab_results.Total IgE: Markedly elevated (often in the thousands IU/mL; may rise further with active infection/eczema)
expected_lab_results.Absolute eosinophil count: Elevated (eosinophilia; may be moderate to severe)
expected_lab_results.WBC: Normal to elevated; may be elevated during acute infection
expected_lab_results.Neutrophils (absolute): Usually normal; may be elevated during acute bacterial infection
expected_lab_results.Hemoglobin: Often normal; may be mildly low with chronic inflammation/infection
expected_lab_results.Platelets: Normal to elevated (reactive thrombocytosis possible during inflammation)
expected_lab_results.CRP: Normal when well; elevated during bacterial infection/flare
expected_lab_results.ESR: Normal when well; elevated during inflammation/infection

medications: Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole (TMP component) 5 mg/kg/day PO divided BID for prophylaxis against recurrent skin/respiratory bacterial infections
Fluconazole 3-6 mg/kg PO daily for antifungal prophylaxis if recurrent mucocutaneous candidiasis or frequent fungal infections (specialist-directed)
Acyclovir 20 mg/kg PO QID (max 800 mg/dose) for antiviral prophylaxis if recurrent HSV/VZV infections (specialist-directed)
Intravenous immunoglobulin (IVIG) 400-600 mg/kg IV every 3-4 weeks if evidence of impaired humoral immunity/recurrent severe infections (immunology-directed)
non_pharmacological: Urgent evaluation and culture-directed therapy for any acute infection; obtain cultures from skin/respiratory sites when feasible
Skin care regimen if eczema present: daily emollients, gentle cleansing; consider antiseptic measures (e.g., dilute bleach baths) if recurrent skin infections
Vaccination review: ensure routine immunizations; avoid live vaccines if significant combined immunodeficiency is confirmed/suspected (immunology guidance)
Infection prevention counseling: hand hygiene, early presentation for fever/skin lesions, avoid exposure to sick contacts when possible

Referral to clinical immunology/genetics for confirmation, immune workup, and long-term management; consider hematopoietic stem cell transplant evaluation for severe AR hyper-IgE syndromes (e.g., DOCK8) per specialist
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess within 1-2 weeks after initiating prophylaxis or sooner for any signs of acute infection; ongoing immunology follow-up every 3-6 months (or more frequently if unstable)

Red-flag symptoms requiring urgent care (fever, breathing difficulty, rapidly spreading skin infection)
Goal of prophylactic antimicrobials: prevent serious infections and complications
Plan for prompt evaluation and targeted treatment of breakthrough infections
Possible need for IVIG and/or transplant evaluation depending on immune testing and severity
Monitoring plan and expected side effects (e.g., TMP-SMX rash, cytopenias; azoles liver toxicity; acyclovir renal considerations)
Review and document amoxicillin-clavulanate allergy (urticarial rash) and avoid prescribing it
Discuss alternative antibiotics for acute infections (e.g., cephalosporins if appropriate, clindamycin, doxycycline if age-appropriate) guided by culture and allergy history
Shared decision-making about prophylaxis and specialist referrals
Infection prevention strategies and early symptom reporting

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit current symptoms, infection history/pattern (skin/lung/sinus/ear), severity, prior organisms/cultures, prior prophylaxis, vaccination status, eczema/viral/fungal history, family history/consanguinity, and current meds.

Order baseline and confirmatory immunology and infection workup to support diagnosis and guide therapy (CBC w/diff for eosinophilia, IgE/IgG/IgA/IgM, lymphocyte subsets, vaccine titers, neutrophil/functional assays if indicated, CMP/LFT/renal baseline before antimicrobials; cultures/imaging if active infection).
Review results to assess immune phenotype, current infection evidence, and organ function relevant to medication choice/monitoring.
Document hyper-IgE recurrent infection syndrome 5 (AR) as working/confirmed diagnosis based on clinical history and immune testing; note differential if incomplete data.
Perform critical allergy verification before prescribing; specifically confirm amoxicillin-clavulanate urticarial rash and clarify severity/timing; document and avoid the culprit drug.

Perform critical contraindication screening before prescribing prophylaxis or acute therapy (renal/hepatic impairment, pregnancy status if relevant, age restrictions for doxycycline, QT risk for macrolides/azoles, cytopenias for TMP-SMX, G6PD if considering certain agents).
Optional but expected medication interaction check (e.g., azoles with statins/warfarin, TMP-SMX with ACEi/ARB/spironolactone, acyclovir with nephrotoxins).

Initiate prophylactic antimicrobials and/or treat active infection with culture-guided alternatives that avoid amoxicillin-clavulanate; include monitoring labs and rescue plan for breakthrough infections.

Meet all communication milestones: explain diagnosis/inheritance and red flags; explain treatment goals, monitoring, side effects; address allergy concerns and alternatives; lifestyle/skin care and prevention; genetic counseling.
Arrange immunology/infectious disease follow-up, lab monitoring schedule, and contingency plan for urgent evaluation; consider referral for IVIG/transplant evaluation depending on severity/testing.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, safety-netting, and follow-up plan.

minimal_paths.description: Standard optimal pathway: assess, confirm immune phenotype, perform safety checks, start prophylaxis/monitoring, educate, and arrange specialist follow-up.
must_collect: frequency/severity of past infections and hospitalizations
eczema/skin abscess history and fungal/viral infection history
amoxicillin-clavulanate allergy details (urticarial rash; timing; anaphylaxis features absent/present)
family history/consanguinity and known genetic results
baseline renal/hepatic history and pregnancy status if relevant
vaccination history and prior vaccine responses if known
steps: ASK: focused history for recurrent infections, eczema, prior organisms, meds, allergy details, family history
ORDER_LAB: CBC w/diff, quantitative immunoglobulins including IgE, CMP/LFT/renal, lymphocyte subsets, vaccine titers; cultures/imaging if symptomatic
GET_RESULTS: interpret eosinophilia/high IgE and immune deficits; assess organ function
DIAGNOSE: hyper-IgE recurrent infection syndrome 5 (AR) as working/confirmed
CHECK_ALLERGY: verify and document amoxicillin-clavulanate urticarial rash; avoid
CHECK_CONTRAINDICATION: renal/hepatic/cytopenias/age/pregnancy considerations for planned meds
CHECK_INTERACTION: screen interactions based on med list

PRESCRIBE: prophylaxis plan (e.g., TMP-SMX if appropriate; consider antifungal/antiviral prophylaxis if indicated by history/testing) and breakthrough infection plan with alternatives; include lab monitoring
EDUCATE: complete all milestones (diagnosis/inheritance/red flags; treatment goals; side effects/monitoring; alternatives given allergy; prevention/skin care; genetic counseling)
SCHEDULE_FOLLOWUP: immunology/ID referral, genetic counseling/testing, lab monitoring timeline
END: confirm understanding and safety-net instructions

minimal_paths.description: If patient presents with an active skin/respiratory infection: treat immediately with safe empiric therapy while obtaining cultures, then transition to prophylaxis and long-term plan.
must_collect: site and severity of current infection (skin abscess/cellulitis; pneumonia/sinusitis/otitis)
vital danger signs (fever, dyspnea, rapidly spreading erythema, toxicity)
renal/hepatic history and baseline labs if available
steps: ASK: assess severity and red flags; determine need for urgent/emergency care
ORDER_LAB: cultures from site if feasible; CBC/CMP; imaging if pneumonia suspected
GET_RESULTS: review any available data; do not delay urgent treatment when clinically necessary
CHECK_ALLERGY: confirm amoxicillin-clavulanate allergy and avoid
CHECK_CONTRAINDICATION: screen for empiric agent contraindications

PRESCRIBE: empiric non-augmentin regimen appropriate to syndrome and site (e.g., clindamycin or cephalosporin if appropriate; doxycycline only if age-appropriate), then tailor to culture; plan prophylaxis after acute infection control
DIAGNOSE: document hyper-IgE recurrent infection syndrome 5 (AR) as underlying condition driving recurrence

correct_diagnosis.primary: Postsynaptic congenital myasthenic syndrome
acceptable_alternatives: Congenital myasthenic syndrome (postsynaptic endplate disorder)
required_evidence: Fluctuating, fatigable weakness with prominent neck flexor involvement (head drop), often worse with exertion and later in the day
Normal sensory exam and no upper motor neuron signs (argues against hereditary spastic paraplegia)
Absence of myopathic pattern such as fixed weakness with markedly elevated CK or prominent muscle atrophy early (argues against myofibrillar/distal myopathies)
Electrodiagnostic evidence of neuromuscular junction transmission defect (e.g., decrement on low-frequency repetitive nerve stimulation or increased jitter on single-fiber EMG)
No acetylcholine receptor or MuSK antibodies (supports congenital rather than autoimmune myasthenia; if tested)
Genetic confirmation of a postsynaptic CMS gene (e.g., CHRNE/CHRND/CHRNB1/CHRNG, RAPSN, DOK7—recognizing DOK7 is functionally postsynaptic-associated)

expected_lab_results.CMP - Bicarbonate (CO2): Normal; may be mildly elevated if chronic hypoventilation/CO2 retention is present in advanced bulbar/respiratory involvement

Albuterol (salbutamol) 2-4 mg PO two to three times daily (or equivalent regimen) for selected CMS subtypes such as DOK7-related disease, per neuromuscular specialist
non_pharmacological: Avoid succinylcholine and minimize/avoid neuromuscular blocking agents when possible; if anesthesia required, use reduced-dose nondepolarizing agents with quantitative neuromuscular monitoring and have reversal strategy planned
Pulmonary function assessment (FVC, NIF) and referral to respiratory therapy if any dyspnea, orthopnea, or sleep-disordered breathing symptoms; consider nocturnal oximetry/capnography
Swallow evaluation if bulbar symptoms; aspiration precautions
Physical therapy focusing on energy conservation, posture/neck support, and graded strengthening without overfatigue
Genetic counseling and family screening where appropriate
Avoid medications that can worsen neuromuscular transmission (e.g., aminoglycosides, fluoroquinolones, magnesium, certain beta-blockers) unless essential and closely monitored
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Neuromuscular specialist follow-up within 2-4 weeks for treatment titration and review of electrodiagnostics/genetic testing; earlier (within 24-72 hours) if respiratory or bulbar symptoms emerge or worsen.

must_include: Condition is a genetic neuromuscular junction disorder causing fatigable weakness; not autoimmune myasthenia gravis
Neck flexor weakness/head drop can be a prominent feature; symptoms fluctuate with exertion

Reinforce penicillin allergy documentation (urticarial rash) and to report any new drug reactions

Vaccination and infection prevention since intercurrent illness can worsen weakness
Family planning/genetic counseling options

Required critical safety check before any prescribing; verify/document penicillin allergy and any other allergies.
Non-critical but expected before prescribing: screen for interactions with planned agents (e.g., QT-prolonging drugs with amifampridine; beta-agonists with stimulants/arrhythmia risk).

If any additional prescriptions are made later in the encounter (e.g., antibiotics), re-check allergy status before prescribing.

Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis and differentials, explain treatment and anesthesia precautions, address red flags and medication avoidance, and provide lifestyle/genetic counseling guidance.
Arrange timely neuromuscular follow-up to review EMG/genetic results, assess treatment response/side effects, and plan respiratory/bulbar monitoring; provide urgent return precautions.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, safety-netting, and follow-up plan.

expected_lab_results.Serum cortisol (8 AM): Low (below normal morning range; typically <3–5 mcg/dL strongly suggests adrenal insufficiency)
expected_lab_results.Plasma ACTH: Low or inappropriately normal (not elevated) consistent with secondary/central adrenal insufficiency
expected_lab_results.Cosyntropin stimulation test (250 mcg) -: Inadequate rise; peak cortisol below assay-specific cutoff (commonly <18 mcg/dL by older assays or below lab-specific threshold), particularly in chronic central adrenal insufficiency
expected_lab_results.TSH: Low or inappropriately normal (central hypothyroidism pattern)

expected_lab_results.1,25-dihydroxyvitamin D: Normal to elevated (PTH-driven conversion may increase level)
expected_lab_results.Creatinine (serum): Elevated consistent with renal insufficiency (reduced eGFR expected)
expected_lab_results.BUN: Often elevated consistent with renal insufficiency/dehydration

Crampy belly discomfort and bloating

general_concerns: I thought pancreatic tumors always cause severe pain right away—since my pain isn’t constant, I worry doctors might be missing something or it can’t be serious.
If my bowels are quiet, it must mean my intestines are shutting down and I could suddenly stop digesting food.
I read online that neuroendocrine tumors are basically the same as pancreatic cancer and always spread quickly, so treatment probably won’t help.
I’m afraid imaging or biopsies can ‘stir up’ the tumor and make it spread.
If stress makes my symptoms worse, maybe this is mostly anxiety or IBS and not a real medical problem.
economic_concerns: I’m worried that specialized tests and scans will be repeated and I’ll be billed each time, so I’d rather skip some steps if possible.
I assume newer treatments are only for people who can pay out-of-pocket, and insurance won’t cover them unless I’m already very sick.
If I need time off for appointments, I’m afraid I’ll lose my job or benefits, so I want the fastest plan even if it’s not the best.

patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 52-year-old anxious but clear-speaking accounting clerk. Start by saying: “I can’t stop having watery diarrhea, and I’m getting really weak.” Volunteer early: watery diarrhea many times daily for weeks, wakes you at night; intense thirst/lightheadedness on standing; crampy bloating; “my stomach feels really quiet/less rumbling.” If asked, add: unintentional weight loss; dry mouth, less urine; occasional racing heart; muscle weakness/leg cramps; foggy/confused on bad days; home BP lower; nausea without vomiting; urgent care said bowel sounds “very quiet.” Do not mention unless specifically asked about meds/OTC: increased antacids/acid blockers, drinking lots of sports drinks/water without relief, taking more anti-diarrhea pills than label, and episodes of tingling/cramping hands after severe diarrhea. Resist/embarrassed: accidents, low sex drive, avoiding meals with others—share only if asked about impact on life. Express misconceptions: think food allergy (mild lip puffiness, no hives/wheeze), “cholesterol problem,” or calcium issue. Voice cost/job worries; push for fastest plan. If clinician shows empathy, you calm slightly, thank them, and become more willing to answer sensitive questions.

Omeprazole 20 mg PO daily
Ondansetron ODT 4 mg PO every 8 hours as needed for nausea
confounders: Chronic proton pump inhibitor use (can elevate chromogranin A and confound neuroendocrine tumor markers)
Dehydration/volume depletion from poor oral intake (contributes to tachycardia, borderline hypotension, elevated BUN)
Electrolyte disturbance (hypokalemia with metabolic alkalosis) that can reduce bowel motility and mimic/compound hypoactive bowel sounds
Recent use of ondansetron (can contribute to constipation/ileus-like symptoms)

correct_diagnosis.primary: Pancreatic polypeptide–secreting pancreatic neuroendocrine tumor (PPoma)
acceptable_alternatives: Nonfunctional pancreatic neuroendocrine tumor with elevated pancreatic polypeptide
Pancreatic neuroendocrine tumor (pNET), pancreatic polypeptide–predominant secretion
required_evidence: Markedly elevated fasting serum pancreatic polypeptide on at least two separate fasting measurements

Supportive neuroendocrine marker elevation (e.g., chromogranin A) with other functional hormone syndromes not predominant (normal/near-normal fasting gastrin, VIP, glucagon, fasting insulin as clinically appropriate)
Symptoms are nonspecific/absent or mass-effect related; no classic syndrome for gastrinoma/VIPoma/glucagonoma/insulinoma driving presentation
Exclusion of common alternative explanations for elevated chromogranin A (e.g., proton pump inhibitor use, significant renal impairment) when interpreting results

expected_lab_results.Fasting plasma glucose: Typically normal; may be mildly elevated if insulin resistance or diabetes is present but not diagnostic for PPoma
expected_lab_results.Hemoglobin A1c: Often normal; may be mildly elevated if comorbid dysglycemia
expected_lab_results.Fasting serum pancreatic polypeptide: Markedly elevated above the reference range; persistent elevation on repeat fasting testing
expected_lab_results.Chromogranin A: Elevated (supportive of neuroendocrine tumor), noting potential false elevation with PPI use or renal dysfunction
expected_lab_results.Serum gastrin (fasting): Normal (helps exclude gastrinoma as primary syndrome)
expected_lab_results.Insulin (fasting): Normal (helps exclude insulinoma in the absence of hypoglycemia)

medications: Octreotide LAR 20-30 mg IM every 4 weeks
non_pharmacological: Order dedicated pancreatic/abdominal imaging for localization and staging (contrast-enhanced multiphasic CT or MRI pancreas); consider somatostatin receptor imaging (e.g., Ga-68 DOTATATE PET/CT) if available for staging and therapy planning
Refer to surgical oncology/hepatopancreatobiliary surgery for evaluation of resectability; surgical resection if localized/resectable

If metastatic/unresectable: multidisciplinary tumor board; consider liver-directed therapy when appropriate and systemic options (e.g., everolimus, sunitinib, peptide receptor radionuclide therapy) based on receptor status and disease burden
Assess for MEN1 if clinically suggested (personal/family history); coordinate genetic counseling/testing as appropriate
Monitor tumor markers and symptoms over time to assess response (pancreatic polypeptide and chromogranin A with consistent pre-analytic conditions)
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess in 4-6 weeks after initiating somatostatin analog (or sooner if symptomatic), then every 3-6 months with labs and interval imaging per staging and response

must_include: PPoma is a pancreatic neuroendocrine tumor characterized by elevated pancreatic polypeptide
Symptoms can be minimal or nonspecific; diagnosis relies on labs plus imaging and sometimes biopsy
Other hormone-secreting tumors are being ruled out with gastrin/VIP/glucagon/insulin testing
If localized, surgery offers the best chance of cure
Somatostatin analog therapy (e.g., octreotide LAR) can help control tumor growth and neuroendocrine activity
Next steps include staging imaging and specialist referral; treatment depends on whether disease is localized or metastatic

Clarify that penicillin allergy is noted and will be avoided if antibiotics are needed (e.g., perioperative planning)
Provide expectations about timelines for imaging, referrals, and follow-up
Encourage adherence to follow-up and imaging schedule

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit key history for suspected pancreatic neuroendocrine tumor/PPoma: nonspecific symptoms, hormonal syndrome red flags, duration, weight change, pain, diarrhea/flushing, hypoglycemia symptoms, prior imaging, family history (MEN1), comorbidities (diabetes), meds, pregnancy status if relevant.

Order confirmatory and differential labs: pancreatic polypeptide, chromogranin A (with PPI use caveat), fasting glucose/A1c, CMP/LFTs, CBC; rule out other functional NETs with gastrin, insulin/C-peptide, glucagon, VIP as appropriate; consider 24h urine/serum 5-HIAA if carcinoid symptoms.
Interpret lab results to support PPoma and exclude other hormone-secreting tumors; assess metabolic status and baseline liver function for staging/therapy planning.

Order/trigger staging workup via appropriate orders (if imaging is represented as labs in this benchmark): pancreas protocol CT or MRI, and somatostatin receptor imaging (Ga-68 DOTATATE PET/CT) for staging; consider biopsy planning if imaging is equivocal or to confirm grade.
Review imaging/staging results to determine localized vs metastatic disease and guide treatment plan and referral urgency.
State working diagnosis (PPoma/pancreatic neuroendocrine tumor with elevated pancreatic polypeptide) and differential considerations; document whether localized vs metastatic based on results.
Perform allergy check before any prescribing, explicitly noting penicillin allergy and implications for perioperative/prophylactic antibiotics.
Check potential drug-drug interactions before prescribing somatostatin analogs and any supportive meds; noncritical but expected if prescribing.

If appropriate (e.g., symptomatic, unresectable/metastatic, or as bridging therapy), prescribe somatostatin analog therapy (e.g., octreotide LAR) or document deferral pending specialist plan; ensure contraindications are considered.

Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis, explain treatment options (surgery vs medical therapy), address concerns (side effects, allergy avoidance, timelines), and provide lifestyle/monitoring guidance.
Arrange oncology/endocrinology and surgical referral, imaging follow-up, lab monitoring (glucose, gallbladder symptoms), and clear return precautions.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, plan, and follow-up.

expected_lab_results.MCV: Normal (normocytic) most commonly; can be mildly low if concurrent iron deficiency
expected_lab_results.Platelets: Normal to elevated (reactive thrombocytosis possible with inflammation)
expected_lab_results.Creatinine: Usually normal; may be elevated if renal artery stenosis/ischemic nephropathy present
expected_lab_results.BUN: Usually normal; may be elevated with renal involvement or dehydration

Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole 800/160 mg PO three times weekly for Pneumocystis jirovecii prophylaxis if on prolonged high-dose glucocorticoids and/or additional immunosuppression (if no sulfa allergy and per local protocol)
Calcium carbonate 1200 mg elemental/day PO in divided doses plus Vitamin D3 800-1000 IU PO daily for bone protection while on glucocorticoids
Omeprazole 20 mg PO daily if high GI-risk on steroids (individualize)
non_pharmacological: Urgent referral to Rheumatology for confirmation and immunosuppressive management
Baseline and follow-up vascular imaging (CTA/MRA or PET-CT) to document extent and activity; repeat imaging in 3-6 months or per specialist

Counsel on infection precautions and prompt evaluation for fever or new symptoms
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess within 1-2 weeks after starting glucocorticoids, then every 4-6 weeks during taper with ESR/CRP monitoring; imaging reassessment in ~3-6 months or sooner if worsening.

patient.instructions:
You are ZhangXX, a 43-year-old male regional sales manager. Be vague and slightly evasive, minimizing details unless directly asked. Start by volunteering: tight/heavy chest pressure with fast walking or stairs, relieved by resting a few minutes; sometimes radiates to left shoulder/jaw; associated shortness of breath; more fatigue lately.

If asked about timing/trends, admit it’s more frequent over 2 weeks, you tolerate less exertion, and once or twice happened during stress at rest. If asked about associated symptoms, mention mild sweating, slight nausea, pounding heartbeat. If asked about relief/meds, say you tried a family member’s nitro once and it helped. If asked about family history, disclose early heart disease (dad MI in 40s, sister procedure in 30s).

Do not volunteer bruising/gum bleeding, low blood counts/transfusions, lightheadedness, leg swelling, or waking short of breath unless specifically asked.

Resist disclosing smoking/vaping, cocaine use, and stopping cholesterol/heart meds unless asked directly; show embarrassment.

Express misconceptions: “Maybe it’s heartburn, a pulled muscle, anxiety, or allergies,” and skepticism about “upsold” tests. Worry about insurance documentation and missing work. If clinician shows empathy, briefly soften, then return to wanting minimal testing and quick solutions.

comorbidities: Coronary artery disease with strong family history consistent with autosomal dominant inheritance
confounders: Fanconi anemia with pancytopenia and thrombocytopenia increases bleeding risk and may limit antiplatelet/anticoagulant options
Anemia can cause exertional chest discomfort and tachycardia, mimicking/worsening angina
Recent or recurrent infections due to neutropenia can cause tachycardia and nonspecific chest symptoms
Possible use of PDE-5 inhibitors (e.g., sildenafil) for erectile dysfunction would contraindicate nitrates and complicate chest pain management
Strong inherited lipid disorder phenotype (elevated LDL-C, ApoB, and Lp(a)) may drive early-onset CAD despite age

correct_diagnosis.primary: Autosomal dominant familial hypercholesterolemia (heterozygous FH) with established coronary artery disease (ASCVD)
acceptable_alternatives: Familial hypercholesterolemia with premature coronary artery disease
Genetic dyslipidemia (likely FH) causing premature ASCVD
Severe primary hypercholesterolemia with elevated lipoprotein(a) and CAD
required_evidence: Established coronary artery disease (ASCVD) requiring secondary prevention intensity lipid lowering
Markedly elevated LDL-C and/or apolipoprotein B consistent with familial hypercholesterolemia phenotype
Autosomal dominant inheritance pattern (family history of premature ASCVD and/or very high LDL-C across generations)
Exclude acute myocardial infarction if presenting with chest pain using serial high-sensitivity troponin I (0 and 3 hours) and clinical assessment

expected_lab_results.High-sensitivity troponin I (0 hr): Not elevated (below assay 99th percentile) or low-level baseline without dynamic rise; if chest pain present, does not meet rule-in criteria at 0 hr
expected_lab_results.High-sensitivity troponin I (3 hr): No significant rise/fall compared with 0 hr (delta below assay-specific rule-in threshold), supporting no acute MI
expected_lab_results.Total cholesterol: Markedly elevated (often >300 mg/dL) consistent with FH
expected_lab_results.LDL-C: Severely elevated untreated (often >=190 mg/dL; frequently >220 mg/dL in heterozygous FH)
expected_lab_results.Triglycerides: Usually normal to mildly elevated (FH typically not severe hypertriglyceridemia)
expected_lab_results.Lipoprotein(a): Often elevated; elevation increases ASCVD risk and supports need for intensive LDL lowering
expected_lab_results.Apolipoprotein B: Elevated, consistent with high atherogenic particle burden

medications: Atorvastatin 80 mg PO daily
Ezetimibe 10 mg PO daily
Evolocumab 140 mg SC every 2 weeks (or Alirocumab 75-150 mg SC every 2 weeks) if LDL-C remains above goal on maximally tolerated statin + ezetimibe
Aspirin 81 mg PO daily (if established CAD and no contraindication/bleeding risk outweighs benefit)
non_pharmacological: Heart-healthy diet (Mediterranean/DASH; reduce saturated fat and trans fat; increase soluble fiber)
Regular aerobic and resistance exercise as tolerated (target >=150 min/week moderate activity)
Smoking cessation and avoidance of secondhand smoke
Weight management and blood pressure/diabetes optimization as applicable
Cascade screening of first-degree relatives for FH (lipids +/- genetic testing where available)
Discuss lipoprotein(a) as a risk enhancer; emphasize aggressive LDL-C lowering as primary strategy
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Repeat fasting lipid panel (including LDL-C and ApoB if used for targets) 4-12 weeks after therapy initiation or intensification, then every 3-12 months once stable; earlier follow-up (4-6 weeks) if adding PCSK9 inhibitor or if adherence/tolerability concerns

must_include: Coronary artery disease means plaque buildup in heart arteries and increases risk of heart attack/stroke
Autosomal dominant familial hypercholesterolemia is a genetic cause of very high LDL from birth; one affected parent can pass it to children with ~50% chance
Current labs indicate high atherogenic cholesterol burden (LDL-C/ApoB) and possibly elevated lipoprotein(a) adding risk
If chest pain prompted testing: serial troponins do not show a heart attack today, but CAD risk remains high and needs aggressive prevention

Expected benefits (reduced MI/stroke risk) and common side effects (statin myalgias, rare liver enzyme elevation; injection site reactions with PCSK9 inhibitors)
Aspirin use rationale in established CAD and discussion of bleeding risk; confirm no history of major bleeding
Plan for monitoring and follow-up lipid testing timeline

Importance of smoking cessation and control of other risk factors (BP, glucose)
Family cascade screening and why early detection/treatment in relatives prevents premature events

correct_diagnosis.primary: Postsynaptic congenital myasthenic syndrome (CMS)
acceptable_alternatives: Congenital myasthenic syndrome (postsynaptic subtype suspected)

required_evidence: Chronic or longstanding fatigable proximal weakness (e.g., hip flexor weakness) with fluctuation and worsening with exertion
Normal sensation and reflexes or only mild reduction without sensory level
No systemic features suggesting inflammatory myopathy (e.g., rash, myalgias) or neuropathy (e.g., numbness/tingling)
History suggestive of early onset or long duration of symptoms and/or family history consistent with inherited disorder
Supportive neuromuscular junction testing when obtained (e.g., decrement on repetitive nerve stimulation or increased jitter on single-fiber EMG)
Exclusion of autoimmune MG when evaluated (e.g., negative AChR/MuSK antibodies) and absence of thymoma features

non_pharmacological: Neuromuscular specialist referral for CMS subtype confirmation (genetic testing) and tailored therapy (some subtypes worsen with acetylcholinesterase inhibitors)
Baseline and periodic functional assessment (e.g., timed up-and-go, stair/hip flexion endurance) and physical therapy focused on energy conservation and proximal strengthening without overfatigue
Avoid known exacerbating medications for myasthenic disorders when possible (e.g., aminoglycosides, fluoroquinolones, macrolides, magnesium-containing products, beta-blockers)
Respiratory symptom screening and consideration of pulmonary function testing if any bulbar/respiratory complaints emerge
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess symptoms, strength, and adverse effects in 2–4 weeks after starting or adjusting therapy; sooner (within 48–72 hours) if worsening weakness, dysphagia, or dyspnea.

Acyclovir 20 mg/kg/dose PO QID (max 800 mg/dose) (if recurrent HSV/VZV or high risk; prophylaxis per immunology)
Cetirizine 10 mg PO daily (or weight-based pediatric dosing) (allergic rhinitis control)
Fluticasone nasal spray 50 mcg/spray: 1-2 sprays per nostril daily (allergic rhinitis control)
non_pharmacological: Refer to clinical immunology for definitive evaluation and longitudinal management (including confirmation of DOCK8 deficiency and immune workup)
Discuss curative intent option: early hematopoietic stem cell transplantation (HSCT) referral/evaluation
Aggressive skin care/eczema management: daily emollients; avoid triggers; prompt evaluation of skin infections
Dust mite avoidance measures for allergic rhinitis (encase bedding, hot-wash linens weekly, reduce bedroom humidity, HEPA filtration if feasible)
Infection prevention counseling: hand hygiene; prompt medical review for fever, skin lesions, respiratory symptoms
Vaccination review with immunology (avoid live vaccines if significant T-cell/combined immunodeficiency suspected/confirmed; ensure inactivated vaccines up to date)
Consider immunoglobulin replacement therapy if there is evidence of impaired antibody responses/recurrent serious infections (per immunology)
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Immunology follow-up within 2-4 weeks (earlier within 48-72 hours if currently symptomatic or on antibiotics); routine monitoring every 3-6 months once stable

must_include: This condition is an inherited immune system disorder (autosomal recessive hyper-IgE syndrome/DOCK8 deficiency) that predisposes to recurrent infections and severe allergies/atopy
High IgE and high eosinophils support the diagnosis but specialist immune testing/genetic confirmation is needed
Red-flag symptoms that require urgent care: fever, rapidly spreading skin redness, breathing difficulty, severe headache/neck stiffness, signs of dehydration
Goal is to prevent infections and control allergic/skin inflammation; prophylactic antimicrobials may be used
HSCT may be a curative option and should be discussed early with immunology
If an infection is suspected, antibiotics must avoid amoxicillin-clavulanate due to immediate allergy; alternative agents will be selected based on site/severity and cultures when possible
Confirm and document the immediate amoxicillin-clavulanate reaction (urticaria and facial swelling) and advise avoidance of this drug; discuss need for allergy/immunology input on beta-lactam use
Discuss potential medication adverse effects (e.g., TMP-SMX rash, cytopenias; acyclovir GI upset) and when to stop/seek care
Explain that dust mite allergy can worsen nasal symptoms and eczema; controlling exposure can reduce flares
Dust mite reduction steps and consistent allergic rhinitis therapy to reduce symptom burden
Skin barrier care routine and early treatment of suspected skin infections
Importance of keeping follow-up appointments and seeking early evaluation for new infections

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit current symptoms, infection history, atopy/allergy history, prior immune workup, current meds, and immediate safety/red flags to determine if urgent care is needed and what evaluation is appropriate.
Perform a critical allergy check before any prescribing; specifically confirm/document immediate reaction to amoxicillin-clavulanate (urticaria/facial swelling) and any other drug/food allergies.

Order basic evaluation consistent with suspected hyper-IgE syndrome/DOCK8 deficiency and recurrent infections (e.g., CBC with differential/eosinophils, total IgE, immunoglobulin panel, lymphocyte subsets as available, culture/viral testing guided by symptoms).
Review results to support suspected diagnosis (elevated IgE/eosinophils) and assess for active infection or complications requiring treatment or escalation.
State likely diagnosis (autosomal recessive hyper-IgE syndrome consistent with DOCK8 deficiency) while noting need for immunology referral and confirmatory immune/genetic testing.
Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis, red flags, treatment goals (infection prevention/atopy control), prophylaxis concept, HSCT discussion, and lifestyle/skin/dust-mite measures.

If treatment is indicated (e.g., suspected infection or prophylaxis plan), prescribe appropriately after safety checks; avoid amoxicillin-clavulanate due to immediate allergy and choose alternatives based on site/severity and cultures when possible.
Arrange immunology/allergy referral, follow-up for lab review and infection monitoring, and reinforce early evaluation for new infections.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, red-flag return precautions, and follow-up plan.

nl_assertions: Agent asks about current infection symptoms and severity (fever, respiratory distress, rapidly spreading skin redness, dehydration, severe headache/neck stiffness) and gives urgent-care instructions when present.
Agent elicits a focused recurrent infection history (skin/respiratory/viral infections, frequency, severity, hospitalizations), eczema/atopy severity, and exposure triggers (including dust mites).

Agent confirms and documents the immediate amoxicillin-clavulanate reaction (urticaria and facial swelling) and advises strict avoidance; recommends allergy/immunology input regarding future beta-lactam use.
Before prescribing any medication, agent completes a critical allergy check and a critical contraindication check; interaction check is performed when relevant but is non-critical.
Agent explains that autosomal recessive hyper-IgE syndrome/DOCK8 deficiency is an inherited immune disorder predisposing to recurrent infections and severe allergies/atopy.
Agent states that high IgE and eosinophilia support the diagnosis but that specialist immune testing and genetic confirmation are needed.

Agent explains treatment goals: prevent infections and control allergic/skin inflammation; mentions prophylactic antimicrobials may be used and that HSCT may be curative and should be discussed early with immunology.
If infection treatment is discussed, agent explicitly avoids amoxicillin-clavulanate and frames antibiotic choice as guided by infection site/severity and cultures when possible.

Agent discusses potential adverse effects and stop/seek-care criteria for commonly used agents in this context (e.g., TMP-SMX rash/cytopenias; acyclovir GI upset), tailored to what is prescribed or proposed.
Agent provides dust mite reduction steps, consistent allergic rhinitis therapy, skin barrier care, and early treatment guidance for suspected skin infections.
Agent avoids overconfident claims of confirmed genetic diagnosis without testing and avoids unsafe empiric prescribing without safety checks.
Agent schedules follow-up and immunology referral and emphasizes early evaluation for new infections.

minimal_paths.description: Stable patient with recurrent infections/atopy; focus on recognition, confirmatory workup, education, and referral without immediate antimicrobial prescribing.
must_collect: prior immune/genetic testing and family history/consanguinity
steps: ASK (history + red flags + meds + allergy details)
ORDER_LAB (CBC diff, total IgE, immunoglobulins +/- lymphocyte subsets as available)
GET_RESULTS (note elevated IgE/eosinophils if present; assess for infection markers if ordered)
DIAGNOSE (suspected AR hyper-IgE/DOCK8; needs immunology/genetics confirmation)
EDUCATE (diagnosis + red flags + prevention + HSCT discussion + dust mite/skin care)

minimal_paths.description: Patient has mild suspected skin infection on background of DOCK8-like phenotype; treat while avoiding amoxicillin-clavulanate and completing safety checks.
must_collect: skin lesion description (location, size, pain, drainage, spreading redness)
contraindication screen relevant to proposed antibiotic (e.g., pregnancy status if applicable, renal disease, G6PD deficiency if considering certain agents)
steps: ASK (focused skin infection history + red flags + meds + allergy details)
ORDER_LAB (culture if purulent; CBC if systemic symptoms; other tests guided by severity)
GET_RESULTS (review any available results; do not delay urgent treatment if severe)
DIAGNOSE (suspected DOCK8 hyper-IgE phenotype + concurrent skin/soft tissue infection)
PRESCRIBE (non-beta-lactam alternative appropriate to severity/local guidance; avoid amoxicillin-clavulanate; provide duration and return precautions)
EDUCATE (red flags, adverse effects, skin care, early follow-up; HSCT/immunology referral)

acceptable_variants.description: Agent performs contraindication check explicitly as a separate step immediately before prescribing (recommended when PRESCRIBE is used).
steps: PRESCRIBE (after explicit contraindication screening in narrative)
